region_of_origin: China (widely consumed across Asia and Singapore)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed green tea, approximately 240 ml, as typically served in a Singaporean coffeeshop or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, leaf, green — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1104262)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are for plain brewed green tea, no milk or sugar added, per 100 g (approximately 100 ml of brewed infusion). This matches the product served at the restaurant as a plain hot green tea beverage.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of beverages — plain brewed green tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates; GI conventionally assigned as effectively zero
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed green tea contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g and therefore has no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a numeric GI to non-caloric beverages. Per Project Veritas v2 zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is set to 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (1 mg per 100 g); contains catechins with mild vasodilatory properties
hypertension.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and antioxidant catechins (EGCG) may support modest blood pressure reduction when consumed regularly as part of a balanced diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no impact on postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea is safe and beneficial for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no glycemic carbohydrates, does not raise blood glucose, and the polyphenol EGCG has been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in clinical studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; rich in catechin antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea is cardioprotective; it contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, and its catechin content (particularly EGCG) has been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and improved endothelial function in epidemiological and intervention studies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green tea (绿茶, Lǜ chá) is a minimally processed beverage made by infusing unoxidised dried green tea leaves in hot water. Unlike black tea, green tea leaves are not fermented, preserving their natural green colour and high polyphenol content, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG). The resulting infusion is pale yellow-green, with a light, slightly grassy, mildly astringent flavour. In Singapore, plain green tea is widely consumed at hawker centres, coffeeshops (kopitiams), and Chinese restaurants, often served hot and unsweetened. It is a staple accompaniment to Chinese cuisine, including braised beef noodle dishes such as those served at Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles. Nutritionally, plain brewed green tea is essentially calorie-free (approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml), contains no protein, fat, or carbohydrate, and has negligible sodium. It is naturally hydrating and contains bioactive compounds including catechins, theanine, and caffeine in modest amounts. Green tea is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals following halal, kosher, or gluten-free diets. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, as it does not raise blood glucose, contains no glycemic carbohydrates, and its antioxidant polyphenols have been associated with cardiovascular and metabolic benefits in multiple peer-reviewed studies.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain brewed green tea (FDC ID 1104262), which is an exact match for this food. The food is a simple, well-characterised beverage with consistent nutritional composition across sources.
notes: Plain brewed green tea is a near-zero-calorie beverage. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed green tea contains no protein in the infusion. fat_g is naturally zero as brewed green tea contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as brewed green tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. energy_kcal is 1 kcal per 100 g per USDA SR Legacy, reflecting trace dissolved solids. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for protein, fat, carbohydrate, and sugar. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Project Veritas v2 zero-calorie beverage glycemic convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed green tea. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed green tea. The food_name in the input was 'Green Tea绿茶'; the canonical English name 'Green Tea' has been used as food_name, with '绿茶' preserved in local_names and aliases. The cuisine context is a Singaporean Chinese braised beef noodle restaurant; multilingual naming covers English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. Japanese (緑茶) is also included given the beverage's pan-Asian identity. Price from field survey data: SGD 1.65.
